modules.title: Getting Started with Rust
modules.focus: Setting up the Rust toolchain and running your first program with Cargo.
objectives: Install Rust using rustup and verify the installation
Create a new project with cargo new and understand the project structure
Write, compile, and run a Hello, World! program
Use essential Cargo commands: build, run, check, and fmt
Understand the role of Cargo.toml in managing dependencies
modules.description: Setting up the Rust toolchain and running your first program with Cargo.

modules.title: Variables, Data Types & Operators
modules.focus: Declaring variables, working with primitive types, and performing operations in Rust.
objectives: Declare variables with let and understand immutability by default
Use mut to create mutable variables and explain why Rust prefers immutability
Identify and use primitive types: integers, floats, booleans, and characters
Apply arithmetic, comparison, and logical operators
Use type inference, explicit type annotations, and variable shadowing
modules.description: Declaring variables, working with primitive types, and performing operations in Rust.

modules.title: Control Flow & Functions
modules.focus: Making decisions with conditionals, repeating code with loops, and organizing logic into functions.
objectives: Write conditional branches using if, else if, and else expressions
Use loop, while, and for to repeat code and control iteration
Define functions with parameters and return values
Distinguish between expressions and statements in Rust
Use basic match expressions to compare values against literal patterns
modules.description: Making decisions with conditionals, repeating code with loops, and organizing logic into functions.

modules.title: Ownership & Borrowing
modules.focus: Understanding Rust's ownership model for memory safety without a garbage collector.
objectives: Explain the three rules of ownership and how values are moved
Use references to borrow values without taking ownership
Distinguish between mutable and immutable borrowing and their rules
Use slices to reference a contiguous sequence of elements in a collection
Recognize common borrow checker errors and understand how to fix them
modules.description: Understanding Rust's ownership model for memory safety without a garbage collector.

modules.title: Structs & Enums
modules.focus: Defining custom data types with structs and enums and adding behavior with methods.
objectives: Define structs with named fields, tuple structs, and unit structs
Implement methods and associated functions using impl blocks
Define enums with variants and attach data to each variant
Use match to handle enum variants and destructure their inner data
Use if let and while let for concise pattern matching on a single variant
modules.description: Defining custom data types with structs and enums and adding behavior with methods.

modules.title: Collections & Strings
modules.focus: Storing multiple values in vectors and hash maps, and working with Rust's string types.
objectives: Create, read, and modify a Vec<T> to store growable lists of values
Distinguish between String and &str and convert between them
Use HashMap<K, V> to store and look up key-value pairs
Iterate over collections and use common methods like push, get, and insert
Understand ownership implications when storing and retrieving data in collections
modules.description: Storing multiple values in vectors and hash maps, and working with Rust's string types.

modules.title: Option, Result & Error Handling
modules.focus: Handling absence and failure safely using Rust's Option and Result types.
objectives: Use Option<T> to represent values that may be absent
Use Result<T, E> to represent operations that may fail
Apply the ? operator to propagate errors concisely
Distinguish between recoverable errors (Result) and unrecoverable errors (panic)
Use match, unwrap, expect, and combinators like map and and_then to handle results
modules.description: Handling absence and failure safely using Rust's Option and Result types.

modules.title: Traits & Generics
modules.focus: Writing flexible, reusable code with traits and generic type parameters.
objectives: Define traits and implement them for custom types
Write generic functions and structs that work with multiple types
Use trait bounds to constrain generic type parameters
Implement common standard traits: Debug, Display, Clone, and PartialEq
Understand trait objects (dyn Trait) as a form of dynamic dispatch
modules.description: Writing flexible, reusable code with traits and generic type parameters.

modules.title: Modules & Code Organization
modules.focus: Organizing Rust code into modules, crates, and packages for maintainability.
objectives: Define modules with mod and understand the module tree
Use use to bring items into scope and create aliases
Control visibility with pub and understand Rust's privacy rules
Distinguish between binary crates and library crates
Add external dependencies in Cargo.toml and use them in code
modules.description: Organizing Rust code into modules, crates, and packages for maintainability.

modules.title: Iterators & Closures
modules.focus: Processing sequences of data with iterators and capturing behavior with closures.
objectives: Define closures and explain how they capture their environment
Understand the Fn, FnMut, and FnOnce traits and when each applies
Create iterators from collections and use the Iterator trait methods
Chain iterator adaptors like map, filter, and take to transform data
Consume iterators with collect, sum, count, and for loops
modules.description: Processing sequences of data with iterators and capturing behavior with closures.

modules.title: Testing & Documentation
modules.focus: Writing and running tests, and generating documentation for Rust code.
objectives: Write unit tests using the #[test] attribute and assert! macros
Organize tests within modules and run them with cargo test
Write integration tests in the tests/ directory
Write doc comments and generate HTML documentation with cargo doc
Use test attributes like #[should_panic] and #[ignore] for specialized cases
modules.description: Writing and running tests, and generating documentation for Rust code.

modules.title: Concurrency Basics
modules.focus: Writing safe concurrent code using threads, message passing, and shared state.
objectives: Spawn threads with std::thread::spawn and move data into them
Use channels (mpsc) to send messages between threads
Share mutable state safely with Mutex<T> and Arc<T>
Explain the Send and Sync marker traits and their role in thread safety
Recognize common concurrency pitfalls and how Rust prevents them at compile time
modules.description: Writing safe concurrent code using threads, message passing, and shared state.

modules.focus: Building a complete command-line application that integrates all learned concepts.
objectives: Design and structure a CLI project using modules and external crates

Use collections, error handling, and file I/O together in a real application
Write and run unit and integration tests for the project
Build, document, and share the project using Cargo

modules.title: Getting Started with Rust
modules.content:
## Why Rust, and Why Start Here?

Imagine you are building a web browser. You need a language that is fast enough to render complex graphics at sixty frames per second, but safe enough that a single malformed webpage doesn't crash the entire application or expose a critical security vulnerability. For decades, developers faced a stark choice: use a language like C or C++ for maximum performance at the cost of safety, or use a language like Python or JavaScript for safety at the cost of raw speed. 

Rust was built to shatter that compromise. It provides low-level control over hardware and memory, comparable to C++, but enforces strict safety rules during compilation that prevent entire classes of bugs before your program ever runs.

But before we can build a web browser, a game engine, or an operating system, we need the tools to write, compile, and run a single line of code. Every Rust programmer—whether building a tiny script or a massive enterprise application—begins the exact same way: by installing the Rust toolchain and creating a "Hello, World!" program.

## Installing the Rust Toolchain

To write and run Rust code, you need a collection of tools known as a **toolchain**. This includes the Rust compiler (the program that translates your human-readable code into machine code), standard libraries, and utility programs. 

Rust uses a fantastic tool called **rustup** to manage this toolchain. Rustup acts as an installer and version manager, ensuring you always have the latest stable version of Rust and allowing you to easily switch between different versions if needed.

### Installing on Windows

If you are using Windows, you will need the C++ build tools. The easiest way to get them is by downloading the "Build Tools for Visual Studio" from Microsoft's official website, which includes the MSVC compiler required to build Rust programs on Windows.

Once the C++ build tools are installed, go to the official Rust website (rustup.rs) and download the `rustup-init.exe` file. Run the executable in your terminal or command prompt, and it will guide you through the installation process.

### Installing on macOS and Linux

For macOS and Linux, the installation is a single command run in your terminal. Open your terminal and paste the following command:

```bash
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
```

This command downloads a script and runs it to install rustup. The script will ask how you want to install Rust; for beginners, simply pressing "Enter" to accept the default installation is the best choice. 

### Verifying the Installation

Once the installation finishes, you need to verify that your computer recognizes the new commands. Close your current terminal window and open a new one (this ensures the terminal reloads its list of available commands). 

Type the following command and press Enter:

```bash
rustc --version
```

If the installation was successful, you will see output similar to this:

```text
rustc 1.75.0 (82e1608df 2023-12-21)
```

The exact numbers will differ depending on the latest version at the time of your installation, but seeing this line confirms that the Rust compiler—named `rustc`—is successfully installed and ready to use.

## Hello, Cargo!

While you *could* write Rust code in a text file and compile it manually using `rustc`, almost no one does this for real projects. Rust comes with a powerful **build tool** and package manager called **Cargo**. 

A build tool automates the process of compiling your code, fetching external libraries, and organizing your files. Cargo handles all the tedious chores of development so you can focus purely on writing logic.

### Creating a New Project

Let's use Cargo to create your very first project. Open your terminal and navigate to the folder where you want to store your code (for example, your Documents folder). Run the following command:

```bash
cargo new hello_rust
```

This command tells Cargo to create a new project directory named `hello_rust`. Navigate into this new folder using the command `cd hello_rust`, and open the folder in your preferred text editor (like VS Code, Sublime, or Notepad). 

### Understanding the Project Structure

If you look inside the `hello_rust` directory, you will see that Cargo did not just create a blank folder. It generated a standard structure for you:

```text
hello_rust/
├── Cargo.toml
└── src/
    └── main.rs
```

Let's break down what these pieces are:

*   **`Cargo.toml`**: This is the configuration file for your project. The `.toml` extension stands for Tom's Obvious, Minimal Language, a format commonly used for configuration files. This file is where you define your project's name, version, and any external libraries it needs to run.
*   **`src/` directory**: This is the designated folder where all your actual Rust source code lives. Keeping code inside `src` keeps your project root clean.
*   **`main.rs`**: Inside the `src` folder, Cargo generated a `main.rs` file. In Rust, a file ending in `.rs` is a Rust source file. The `main.rs` file is special: it is the entry point of your program. When you run your program, the computer always starts executing code from this file.

## Writing Your First Program

Open the `src/main.rs` file in your text editor. Cargo has already populated it with a "Hello, World!" program. Let's look at the code:

```rust
fn main() {
    println!("Hello, world!");
}
```

Even in just three lines, there is a lot to unpack for a beginner:

*   **`fn`**: This is short for "function". It tells Rust we are defining a new function.
*   **`main`**: This is the name of the function. As mentioned, `main` is a special name in Rust; it is the very first code that runs when you execute your program.
*   **`()`**: Parentheses are used to pass information into a function. Our `main` function takes no inputs, so they are empty.
*   **`{ }`**: Curly braces define the "body" of the function. Everything between these braces is the code that belongs to `main`.
*   **`println!`**: This is a **macro** that prints text to your terminal screen. You can think of a macro as a built-in shortcut that writes extra code for you behind the scenes. For now, just know that `println!` displays whatever text you put inside its parentheses. (Note the exclamation mark `!`—this is what tells Rust it is a macro, not a standard function).
*   **`"Hello, world!"`**: Text wrapped in double quotes is called a **string literal**. It represents exact text to be printed.
*   **`;`**: Semicolons mark the end of a statement. They tell the compiler "this specific instruction is done; move to the next one."

## Compiling and Running

Code written in a `.rs` file is just text. To turn it into a working program, it must be **compiled** (translated into machine code). Cargo makes this effortless. 

Make sure your terminal is inside the `hello_rust` directory, and run this command:

```bash
cargo run
```

You will see output in your terminal that looks something like this:

```text
   Compiling hello_rust v0.1.0 (/path/to/your/hello_rust)
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.42s
     Running `target/debug/hello_rust`
Hello, world!
```

Cargo compiled your code and immediately ran the resulting program, outputting "Hello, world!" to the screen. 

### A Real-World Example: A CLI Greeting

Let's modify the code to make it slightly more interactive. Suppose you are building a command-line tool to greet a new employee on their first day. Change your `main.rs` to look like this:

```rust
fn main() {
    println!("Welcome to the team!");
    println!("Your onboarding process begins now.");
}
```

Save the file, and run `cargo run` again. Cargo is smart: it notices you changed the file, recompiles it automatically, and runs the new version. You should see your two new lines of text printed to the screen.

## Essential Cargo Commands

As you develop, you will rely on a handful of Cargo commands constantly. Here are the four most important ones for a beginner.

### `cargo build`

If you want to compile your project *without* running it, you use `cargo build`. This is useful if you just want to check if your code compiles successfully, or if you are preparing the final program to send to someone else.

```bash
cargo build
```

When you run this for the first time, Cargo creates a new folder in your project called `target/`. Inside this folder is the actual executable file (a `.exe` on Windows, or a binary file on macOS/Linux). You do not need to worry about the `target` folder; it is generated automatically and should generally be ignored if you are using version control software like Git.

### `cargo check`

When your projects get large, compiling them can take time. If you just want to know if your code is free of syntax errors without taking the time to generate the final machine code, use `cargo check`.

```bash
cargo check
```

This command runs the Rust compiler's safety and syntax checks but skips the final step of generating the executable. It is significantly faster than `cargo build` and is the command you will use most frequently while actively typing out new code.

### `cargo fmt`

Writing clean, consistently formatted code is vital for collaboration. However, spending time manually fixing indentation and spacing is tedious. `cargo fmt` automatically formats your code to match the official Rust style guidelines.

```bash
cargo fmt
```

If you wrote code that looked messy, running this command will silently rewrite your files to be perfectly aligned and spaced. It requires no configuration.

## Managing Dependencies with `Cargo.toml`

One of Cargo's most powerful features is its ability to manage **dependencies** (often called "crates" in the Rust ecosystem). A dependency is an external library written by someone else that you want to use in your project to avoid reinventing the wheel.

Let’s look at a real-world scenario. Suppose you are building a tool that needs to generate random numbers. Rust’s standard library does not include a random number generator, but the Rust community has published an excellent library called `rand`. 

Open your `Cargo.toml` file. It currently looks like this:

```toml
[package]
name = "hello_rust"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
```

The `[dependencies]` section is where you list external libraries. To add the `rand` library, you modify the file like this:

```toml
[package]
name = "hello_rust"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
rand = "0.8.5"
```

Here, `rand` is the name of the library, and `"0.8.5"` is the version number you want to use. Cargo uses a system called Semantic Versioning, but for now, just know that specifying a version ensures your project always uses a compatible version of that library.

Now, update your `src/main.rs` to use this new library:

```rust
use rand::Rng;

fn main() {
    let secret_number = rand::thread_rng().gen_range(1..=100);
    println!("Your onboarding ID is: {}", secret_number);
}
```

Do not worry if the syntax on the first few lines looks unfamiliar—we will cover variables and importing libraries in later chapters. The key takeaway is the workflow. 

Run the program with `cargo run`. You will see output similar to this:

```text
    Updating crates.io index
  Downloaded rand v0.8.5
  Downloaded 1 crate (1.2 MB) in 0.5s
   Compiling libc v0.2.153
   Compiling cfg-if v1.0.0
   Compiling ppv-lite86 v0.2.17
   Compiling getrandom v0.2.12
   Compiling rand_core v0.6.4
   Compiling rand_chacha v0.2.2
   Compiling rand v0.8.5
   Compiling hello_rust v0.1.0 (/path/to/your/hello_rust)
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 1.8s
     Running `target/debug/hello_rust`
Your onboarding ID is: 42
```

Notice what happened. When you typed `cargo run`, Cargo saw that you added `rand` to your `Cargo.toml`. It automatically reached out to **crates.io** (the official community registry of Rust libraries), downloaded the `rand` library, downloaded any libraries that `rand` itself relies on, compiled all of them, and finally compiled and ran your program. 

This is the magic of Cargo. You never have to manually download zip files, configure include paths, or struggle with linker errors. You simply add a line to `Cargo.toml`, and Cargo handles the rest.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Rustup** is the official installer and version manager for Rust. You verify it is installed by checking `rustc --version`.
*   **Cargo** is Rust’s built-in build tool and package manager. It is the standard way to create, build, and manage Rust projects.
*   Running `cargo new <project_name>` generates a standardized project structure, including a `Cargo.toml` configuration file and a `src/main.rs` file for your code.
*   The `main` function inside `main.rs` is the entry point for every Rust program. The `println!` macro is used to print text to the screen.
*   `cargo run` compiles and executes your code in one step. `cargo build` compiles without running, and `cargo check` rapidly checks for errors without generating an executable.
*   `cargo fmt` automatically formats your code to standard Rust style guidelines.
*   External libraries (called crates) are added to your project by listing them in the `[dependencies]` section of your `Cargo.toml` file. Cargo automatically downloads and compiles them for you.

## Why Test and Document Your Code?

Imagine you write a function to calculate the final price of a shopping cart, applying a 10% discount to items over $50. You run it once, it looks right, and you move on. Three weeks later, you add a new feature for tax calculation. You deploy your updated application, and suddenly, customers are being charged $0 for their entire carts. What happened? 

Without tests, every change to your codebase is a leap of faith. As your Rust programs grow—incorporating structs, enums, traits, and complex ownership patterns—the mental overhead of verifying that every piece still works becomes impossible. 

**Testing** is the practice of writing code that checks your other code. By writing automated tests, you create a safety net. If you change a function and break something, your tests will tell you immediately. **Documentation**, on the other hand, is how you communicate with your future self, your teammates, or anyone else who wants to use your code. 

Rust treats both testing and documentation as first-class citizens. The Rust compiler (`rustc`) and build tool (`Cargo`) have built-in support for running tests and generating HTML documentation directly from your source code.

## Writing Your First Unit Test

A **unit test** is a small test that checks a single, isolated piece of code—usually one function. In Rust, unit tests live in the same file as the code they are testing.

Let’s look at a practical example. Suppose we are building a simple arithmetic module and want to test an addition function.

```rust
// src/math_utils.rs

// This is the function we want to test
pub fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {
    a + b
}

// This module holds our tests
#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    // Bring the parent module's items into scope so we can test them
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    fn it_adds_two_numbers() {
        let result = add(2, 2);
        assert_eq!(result, 4);
    }
}
```

There are a few new concepts here:

- **`#[cfg(test)]`**: This is an **attribute** (a piece of metadata attached to a module). It tells the Rust compiler to only compile this `tests` module when you run `cargo test`, not when you are building your final application for release. This keeps your final binary small and fast.
- **`mod tests`**: We use the module system (covered in Chapter 9) to neatly tuck our tests away.
- **`use super::*`**: `super` refers to the parent module. This line brings the `add` function into the test module's scope so we can use it without typing `super::add` every time.
- **`#[test]`**: This attribute marks the function below it as a test function. When you run `cargo test`, Rust will look for every function with this attribute and run it.
- **`assert_eq!`**: This is a **macro** (like `println!`) that asserts that two values are equal. If `result` is not equal to `4`, the test fails.

### The Assertion Macros

Rust provides three primary macros for making assertions in your tests:

1. **`assert!(expression)`**: Passes if the expression evaluates to `true`. Fails if it evaluates to `false`.
2. **`assert_eq!(left, right)`**: Passes if `left` equals `right`. 
3. **`assert_ne!(left, right)`**: Passes if `left` does *not* equal `right`.

You can also add a custom error message to any assertion macro as an optional final argument. This is helpful for providing context when a test fails.

```rust
#[test]
fn it_checks_truthiness() {
    let is_ready = true;
    assert!(is_ready, "The system was not ready, expected true");
}
```

## Organizing Tests and Running Them with Cargo

In the previous example, we put our tests inside a `tests` module at the bottom of the file. This is the standard convention in Rust. By keeping tests in a submodule, you keep your production code clean while still having tests live right next to the code they verify.

To run your tests, open your terminal and simply type:

```bash
cargo test
```

When you run this command, Cargo does several things:
1. It compiles your project (including the `#[cfg(test)]` modules).
2. It runs every function marked with `#[test]`.
3. It prints a report showing which tests passed and which failed.

The output looks something like this:

```text
   Compiling my_project v0.1.0 (/path/to/my_project)
    Finished test [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.50s
     Running unittests src/lib.rs (target/debug/deps/my_project-12345)

running 1 test
test tests::it_adds_two_numbers ... ok

test result: ok. 1 passed; 0 failed; 0 ignored; 0 measured; 0 filtered out
```

### Filtering Tests

As your project grows, you might have dozens of tests. Running all of them every time you change a single line can be slow. You can run a specific test by passing its name as an argument to `cargo test`:

```bash
cargo test it_adds_two_numbers
```

Cargo will only run the test whose name matches that string.

## Specialized Test Attributes

Not every test is a straightforward "call a function and check the result." Sometimes you need to test how your code handles errors, or you need to write a test that takes a long time to run. Rust provides attributes to handle these cases.

### Testing for Panics with `#[should_panic]`

In Chapter 7, we discussed how Rust handles errors using `Option` and `Result`. However, sometimes code encounters a state it cannot recover from and calls the `panic!` macro, abruptly stopping the program. 

If you write a function that is *supposed* to panic under certain conditions (for example, if someone passes invalid input), you can test it using the `#[should_panic]` attribute.

```rust
// src/math_utils.rs

pub fn divide(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {
    if b == 0 {
        panic!("Cannot divide by zero!");
    }
    a / b
}

#[cfg(test)]
mod tests {
    use super::*;

    #[test]
    #[should_panic(expected = "Cannot divide by zero!")]
    fn it_panics_when_dividing_by_zero() {
        divide(10, 0);
    }
}
```

Here, we stack two attributes: `#[test]` and `#[should_panic]`. Rust will run the function `it_panics_when_dividing_by_zero`. The test only **passes** if the code inside it actually panics. 

The `expected` parameter is optional but highly recommended. If provided, Rust ensures that the panic message contains the specified string. This prevents the test from passing if the function panics for a completely different, unexpected reason.

### Skipping Tests with `#[ignore]`

Sometimes you write a test that is very slow—perhaps it connects to an external database or performs heavy mathematical computations. You don't want it to run every single time you type `cargo test`, but you still want to keep it in your codebase.

You can mark these tests with `#[ignore]`.

```rust
#[test]
#[ignore]
fn slow_performance_test() {
    // Code that takes a long time to run...
    // e.g., sorting a list of 10 million numbers
}
```

When you run `cargo test`, this test will be listed as `ignored` and will not execute. If you want to run *only* the ignored tests, you can run:

```bash
cargo test -- --ignored
```

## Integration Testing

While unit tests are great for checking the microscopic details of your code, **integration tests** are designed to test the larger, external behavior of your library. They are written as if they are an outside user who has imported your code as a dependency.

Integration tests do not live inside your `src/` directory. Instead, they live in a completely separate folder called `tests/` at the root of your project (right next to `src/` and `Cargo.toml`).

To create an integration test, you first need to ensure your project is set up as a library. If you used `cargo new my_project`, Cargo created a binary project with a `src/main.rs` file. Integration tests can only test library crates, so you need to add a `src/lib.rs` file.

Let’s assume you have a library crate with a public `add` function in `src/lib.rs`:

```rust
// src/lib.rs
pub fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {
    a + b
}
```

Now, create a file at `tests/integration_test.rs`:

```rust
// tests/integration_test.rs
use my_project::add; // Importing your crate like an external user would

#[test]
fn test_add_via_integration() {
    assert_eq!(add(5, 7), 12);
}
```

When you run `cargo test`, Cargo will compile your library, run the unit tests inside `src/`, and then compile and run the integration tests inside `tests/`. You will see separate sections in your terminal output for "Running unittests" and "Running tests/integration_test.rs".

Because integration tests are entirely separate files, they do not need the `#[cfg(test)]` attribute or the `mod tests` wrapper. Cargo knows that any `.rs` file in the `tests/` directory is a test file.

## Writing Documentation Comments

Documentation is crucial, but maintaining separate documentation files that describe your code can be tedious—they often get out of date. Rust solves this by letting you write documentation directly in your source code using **doc comments**.

You've already seen regular comments, which start with `//`:

```rust
// This is a regular comment. The compiler ignores it completely.
let x = 5; // Used for internal notes to developers.
```

**Doc comments** use three slashes (`///`) and are written directly above the item (function, struct, enum, etc.) they describe. The Rust compiler reads these and can turn them into HTML documentation.

```rust
/// Adds two integers together.
/// 
/// # Arguments
/// * `a` - The first integer to add.
/// * `b` - The second integer to add.
///
/// # Examples
/// ```
/// let result = my_project::add(2, 3);
/// assert_eq!(result, 5);
/// ```
pub fn add(a: i32, b: i32) -> i32 {
    a + b
}
```

Notice the formatting inside the doc comment. You can use **Markdown** to format your text. Common sections in Rust documentation include:
- **`# Arguments`**: Describes the parameters.
- **`# Returns`**: Describes what the function gives back.
- **`# Panics`**: Documents the conditions under which the function will panic.
- **`# Examples`**: Shows how to use the function.

### Generating HTML with `cargo doc`

Writing doc comments is great, but the real magic happens when you turn them into a website. You can generate beautiful, searchable HTML documentation for your entire project by running:

```bash
cargo doc --open
```

Cargo will use a tool called `rustdoc` to parse your code and your doc comments, generate HTML files, and place them in a `target/doc/` directory. The `--open` flag tells Cargo to automatically open this documentation in your default web browser.

If you have ever looked up how a standard library function works on the official Rust website, you were looking at documentation generated exactly this way!

## Doc-Tests: Testing Your Documentation

You might have noticed that the `# Examples` section in our doc comment contained a block of Rust code. Rust treats these code blocks as **doc-tests**. 

When you run `cargo test`, Rust will actually extract the code blocks from your doc comments, compile them, and run them as tests!

This creates a powerful incentive: if your code changes and the example in your documentation no longer works, your `cargo test` command will fail. This ensures your documentation is never out of date.

Let's look at how a doc-test failure appears. If we changed the `add` function to subtract instead, but forgot to update our doc comment, `cargo test` would output something like this:

```text
running 1 test
test src/lib.rs - add (line 1) ... FAILED

failures:

---- src/lib.rs - add (line 1) stdout ----
error: assertion `left == right` failed
  left: -1
 right: 5
```

Doc-tests are a fantastic way to ensure that the examples you provide to your users actually compile and run as advertised. 

*Note: While doc-tests are run by `cargo test`, they are compiled and executed slightly differently than standard unit tests. They are run in a separate process, and the code block is treated as a standalone program that imports your crate. Because of this, doc-tests are slightly slower to run than unit tests, but the benefit of always-correct documentation usually outweighs the extra time.*

## Key Takeaways

- **Testing is built into Rust**: You don't need external testing frameworks to get started. Cargo handles test compilation and execution natively.
- **Unit tests** live in `src/` inside a `#[cfg(test)] mod tests` block, right next to the code they verify. They use `use super::*;` to access the parent module.
- **`cargo test`** discovers all functions marked with the `#[test]` attribute and runs them. You can filter tests by passing a name string to this command.
- **Assertion macros** (`assert!`, `assert_eq!`, `assert_ne!`) are the primary way to verify that your code produces the expected output.
- **`#[should_panic]`** allows you to test that your code correctly panics and aborts when given invalid input or encountering an unrecoverable state.
- **`#[ignore]`** is used to skip long-running tests during normal development, keeping your `cargo test` feedback loop fast.
- **Integration tests** live in the `tests/` directory at the project root. They test the library's public API exactly as an external user would experience it.
- **Doc comments** (`///`) use Markdown to describe your code and are placed directly above the item they document.
- **`cargo doc --open`** generates an HTML website from your doc comments, providing a navigable reference for your codebase.
- **Doc-tests** automatically extract and run code blocks found in your doc comments, ensuring your examples never silently break.

## Introduction

### Welcome and Purpose

Welcome aboard! If you have picked up this book, you are likely standing at the threshold of an exciting new journey. You might be a student eager to build your first real-world application, a hobbyist looking to level up your coding toolkit, or a seasoned developer in another language feeling the pull of something modern, fast, and remarkably safe. Whatever your background, I am thrilled to have you here. 

Learning a new programming language can feel like standing at the base of a towering mountain. But let me assure you: Rust is a mountain well worth climbing. Over the past few years, Rust has captured the hearts of developers worldwide, consistently topping the most loved programming languages in developer surveys. Why? Because Rust offers a rare, almost magical promise: it provides the blistering, low-level performance of languages like C and C++, but without the looming threat of crashes, memory leaks, and security vulnerabilities. 

The purpose of this book is simple: to be your patient, knowledgeable guide as you learn Rust from absolute scratch. I remember what it felt like to be a beginner, staring at cryptic compiler errors and wondering if I was just cut out for this. You are. Rust has a reputation for a steep learning curve, but that reputation often stems from trying to run before walking. Here, we will walk together. We will take small, deliberate steps, celebrating the small victories and demystifying the concepts that seem intimidating at first glance. By the end of this journey, you will not just know Rust syntax; you will think in Rust.

### What Readers Will Learn

Rust is a multi-paradigm system programming language focused on safety, speed, and concurrency. But what does that actually mean for you as a beginner? 

Throughout this book, you will learn how to write programs that are not only incredibly fast but also provably correct. You will discover how to work with variables, data types, and operators to build the foundational logic of your software. You will learn how to control the flow of your programs and encapsulate logic into reusable functions. 

But we won’t stop at the basics. You will uncover the secret sauce of Rust: its unique approach to memory management through **Ownership and Borrowing**. You will learn how to model complex, real-world data using Structs and Enums, and how to manage groups of data using Collections and Strings. 

Mistakes happen, but in Rust, you will learn how to handle them gracefully using the powerful `Option` and `Result` types. You will master Traits and Generics to write flexible, reusable code, and organize your growing codebases elegantly using Modules. As you advance, you will discover the elegance of Iterators and Closures, ensure your code is rock-solid with Testing and Documentation, and finally, dip your toes into the thrilling world of safe Concurrency. 

Ultimately, you will learn to write code that you can be proud of—code that runs efficiently, securely, and exactly as intended.

### Book Structure

To make this learning process smooth and engaging, this book is structured as a progressive roadmap. Each chapter builds upon the last, ensuring you are never overwhelmed by new concepts. Here is a sneak peek at the path we will take together:

- **Getting Started with Rust:** We will set up your development environment, install Rust via the `rustup` toolchain, and write our very first "Hello, World!" program. You will also meet `cargo`, Rust’s incredible build system and package manager.
- **Variables, Data Types & Operators:** The building blocks of any language. We will explore mutability, scalar types, compound types, and how to perform operations on them.
- **Control Flow & Functions:** Here, we will learn how to make decisions in code using `if/else` statements, loop over data, and encapsulate our logic into reusable functions.
- **Ownership & Borrowing:** The defining feature of Rust. We will tackle this head-on, learning how Rust manages memory without a garbage collector, and how to borrow data safely.
- **Structs & Enums:** We will learn how to model real-world entities, group related data together, and give our programs a meaningful structure.
- **Collections & Strings:** We will explore how to store multiple values in Vectors, manage key-value pairs in HashMaps, and navigate Rust’s unique approach to Strings.
- **Option, Result & Error Handling:** We will learn how Rust handles the absence of values and unexpected errors without relying on unpredictable exceptions.
- **Traits & Generics:** You will discover how to define shared behavior and write code that works with multiple data types while maintaining strict type safety.
- **Modules & Code Organization:** As our programs grow, we will learn how to split them into manageable files and modules, keeping our projects clean and navigable.
- **Iterators & Closures:** We will explore Rust’s functional programming features, learning how to process data elegantly and concisely.
- **Testing & Documentation:** We will cover how to write unit tests to ensure your code works, and how to generate beautiful documentation for your projects.
- **Concurrency Basics:** We will touch upon threads and asynchronous programming, showing you how Rust makes concurrent programming safe and fearless.
- **Capstone Project:** Finally, we will bring everything together. You will build a complete, functional Rust application from scratch, cementing your new skills and giving you a tangible portfolio piece.

### Motivation

You might be wondering, "With so many programming languages out there, why should I invest my time in Rust?" 

It is a fair question. If you want to build a quick website, JavaScript might be faster. If you want to glue together some data scripts, Python is wonderful. But if you want to build systems that are fast, reliable, and safe from the ground up, Rust is in a league of its own. 

Rust empowers you to write code that runs at the speed of light, directly interacting with hardware, without the constant fear of crashing your system. The Rust compiler, affectionately known as the "Borrow Checker," is not your enemy—it is your strictest, most helpful mentor. It will refuse to let you compile code that contains memory bugs. While this might feel frustrating at first, it is incredibly liberating. When a Rust program finally compiles, it often "just works." The dreaded "null pointer exceptions" and "segmentation faults" that plague other low-level languages are virtually eliminated.

Furthermore, the Rust community is one of the most welcoming, inclusive, and helpful communities in tech. From the official documentation to community forums, you will find a wealth of support. 

Learning Rust is an investment in your future as a developer. It will change the way you think about memory, concurrency, and software design. Even if you go back to writing Python, JavaScript, or C# after this book, the mental models you develop in Rust will make you a better, more thoughtful programmer in any language.

So, take a deep breath. Roll up your sleeves. You are about to embark on a journey that will challenge you, excite you, and ultimately transform you into a confident, fearless systems programmer. Let’s learn Rust!

# Chapter 1: Getting Started with Rust

## Why Rust, and Why Start Here?

Imagine you are building a web browser. You need a language that is fast enough to render complex graphics at sixty frames per second, but safe enough that a single malformed webpage doesn't crash the entire application or expose a critical security vulnerability. For decades, developers faced a stark choice: use a language like C or C++ for maximum performance at the cost of safety, or use a language like Python or JavaScript for safety at the cost of raw speed. 

Rust was built to shatter that compromise. It provides low-level control over hardware and memory, comparable to C++, but enforces strict safety rules during compilation that prevent entire classes of bugs before your program ever runs.

But before we can build a web browser, a game engine, or an operating system, we need the tools to write, compile, and run a single line of code. Every Rust programmer—whether building a tiny script or a massive enterprise application—begins the exact same way: by installing the Rust toolchain and creating a "Hello, World!" program.

## Installing the Rust Toolchain

To write and run Rust code, you need a collection of tools known as a **toolchain**. This includes the Rust compiler (the program that translates your human-readable code into machine code), standard libraries, and utility programs. 

Rust uses a fantastic tool called **rustup** to manage this toolchain. Rustup acts as an installer and version manager, ensuring you always have the latest stable version of Rust and allowing you to easily switch between different versions if needed.

### Installing on Windows

If you are using Windows, you will need the C++ build tools. The easiest way to get them is by downloading the "Build Tools for Visual Studio" from Microsoft's official website, which includes the MSVC compiler required to build Rust programs on Windows.

Once the C++ build tools are installed, go to the official Rust website (rustup.rs) and download the `rustup-init.exe` file. Run the executable in your terminal or command prompt, and it will guide you through the installation process.

### Installing on macOS and Linux

For macOS and Linux, the installation is a single command run in your terminal. Open your terminal and paste the following command:

```bash
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
```

This command downloads a script and runs it to install rustup. The script will ask how you want to install Rust; for beginners, simply pressing "Enter" to accept the default installation is the best choice. 

### Verifying the Installation

Once the installation finishes, you need to verify that your computer recognizes the new commands. Close your current terminal window and open a new one (this ensures the terminal reloads its list of available commands). 

Type the following command and press Enter:

```bash
rustc --version
```

If the installation was successful, you will see output similar to this:

```text
rustc 1.75.0 (82e1608df 2023-12-21)
```

The exact numbers will differ depending on the latest version at the time of your installation, but seeing this line confirms that the Rust compiler—named `rustc`—is successfully installed and ready to use.

## Hello, Cargo!

While you *could* write Rust code in a text file and compile it manually using `rustc`, almost no one does this for real projects. Rust comes with a powerful **build tool** and package manager called **Cargo**. 

A build tool automates the process of compiling your code, fetching external libraries, and organizing your files. Cargo handles all the tedious chores of development so you can focus purely on writing logic.

### Creating a New Project

Let's use Cargo to create your very first project. Open your terminal and navigate to the folder where you want to store your code (for example, your Documents folder). Run the following command:

```bash
cargo new hello_rust
```

This command tells Cargo to create a new project directory named `hello_rust`. Navigate into this new folder using the command `cd hello_rust`, and open the folder in your preferred text editor (like VS Code, Sublime, or Notepad). 

### Understanding the Project Structure

If you look inside the `hello_rust` directory, you will see that Cargo did not just create a blank folder. It generated a standard structure for you:

```text
hello_rust/
├── Cargo.toml
└── src/
    └── main.rs
```

Let's break down what these pieces are:

*   **`Cargo.toml`**: This is the configuration file for your project. The `.toml` extension stands for Tom's Obvious, Minimal Language, a format commonly used for configuration files. This file is where you define your project's name, version, and any external libraries it needs to run.
*   **`src/` directory**: This is the designated folder where all your actual Rust source code lives. Keeping code inside `src` keeps your project root clean.
*   **`main.rs`**: Inside the `src` folder, Cargo generated a `main.rs` file. In Rust, a file ending in `.rs` is a Rust source file. The `main.rs` file is special: it is the entry point of your program. When you run your program, the computer always starts executing code from this file.

## Writing Your First Program

Open the `src/main.rs` file in your text editor. Cargo has already populated it with a "Hello, World!" program. Let's look at the code:

```rust
fn main() {
    println!("Hello, world!");
}
```

Even in just three lines, there is a lot to unpack for a beginner:

*   **`fn`**: This is short for "function". It tells Rust we are defining a new function.
*   **`main`**: This is the name of the function. As mentioned, `main` is a special name in Rust; it is the very first code that runs when you execute your program.
*   **`()`**: Parentheses are used to pass information into a function. Our `main` function takes no inputs, so they are empty.
*   **`{ }`**: Curly braces define the "body" of the function. Everything between these braces is the code that belongs to `main`.
*   **`println!`**: This is a **macro** that prints text to your terminal screen. You can think of a macro as a built-in shortcut that writes extra code for you behind the scenes. For now, just know that `println!` displays whatever text you put inside its parentheses. (Note the exclamation mark `!`—this is what tells Rust it is a macro, not a standard function).
*   **`"Hello, world!"`**: Text wrapped in double quotes is called a **string literal**. It represents exact text to be printed.
*   **`;`**: Semicolons mark the end of a statement. They tell the compiler "this specific instruction is done; move to the next one."

## Compiling and Running

Code written in a `.rs` file is just text. To turn it into a working program, it must be **compiled** (translated into machine code). Cargo makes this effortless. 

Make sure your terminal is inside the `hello_rust` directory, and run this command:

```bash
cargo run
```

You will see output in your terminal that looks something like this:

```text
   Compiling hello_rust v0.1.0 (/path/to/your/hello_rust)
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 0.42s
     Running `target/debug/hello_rust`
Hello, world!
```

Cargo compiled your code and immediately ran the resulting program, outputting "Hello, world!" to the screen. 

### A Real-World Example: A CLI Greeting

Let's modify the code to make it slightly more interactive. Suppose you are building a command-line tool to greet a new employee on their first day. Change your `main.rs` to look like this:

```rust
fn main() {
    println!("Welcome to the team!");
    println!("Your onboarding process begins now.");
}
```

Save the file, and run `cargo run` again. Cargo is smart: it notices you changed the file, recompiles it automatically, and runs the new version. You should see your two new lines of text printed to the screen.

## Essential Cargo Commands

As you develop, you will rely on a handful of Cargo commands constantly. Here are the four most important ones for a beginner.

### `cargo build`

If you want to compile your project *without* running it, you use `cargo build`. This is useful if you just want to check if your code compiles successfully, or if you are preparing the final program to send to someone else.

```bash
cargo build
```

When you run this for the first time, Cargo creates a new folder in your project called `target/`. Inside this folder is the actual executable file (a `.exe` on Windows, or a binary file on macOS/Linux). You do not need to worry about the `target` folder; it is generated automatically and should generally be ignored if you are using version control software like Git.

### `cargo check`

When your projects get large, compiling them can take time. If you just want to know if your code is free of syntax errors without taking the time to generate the final machine code, use `cargo check`.

```bash
cargo check
```

This command runs the Rust compiler's safety and syntax checks but skips the final step of generating the executable. It is significantly faster than `cargo build` and is the command you will use most frequently while actively typing out new code.

### `cargo fmt`

Writing clean, consistently formatted code is vital for collaboration. However, spending time manually fixing indentation and spacing is tedious. `cargo fmt` automatically formats your code to match the official Rust style guidelines.

```bash
cargo fmt
```

If you wrote code that looked messy, running this command will silently rewrite your files to be perfectly aligned and spaced. It requires no configuration.

## Managing Dependencies with `Cargo.toml`

One of Cargo's most powerful features is its ability to manage **dependencies** (often called "crates" in the Rust ecosystem). A dependency is an external library written by someone else that you want to use in your project to avoid reinventing the wheel.

Let’s look at a real-world scenario. Suppose you are building a tool that needs to generate random numbers. Rust’s standard library does not include a random number generator, but the Rust community has published an excellent library called `rand`. 

Open your `Cargo.toml` file. It currently looks like this:

```toml
[package]
name = "hello_rust"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
```

The `[dependencies]` section is where you list external libraries. To add the `rand` library, you modify the file like this:

```toml
[package]
name = "hello_rust"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[dependencies]
rand = "0.8.5"
```

Here, `rand` is the name of the library, and `"0.8.5"` is the version number you want to use. Cargo uses a system called Semantic Versioning, but for now, just know that specifying a version ensures your project always uses a compatible version of that library.

Now, update your `src/main.rs` to use this new library:

```rust
use rand::Rng;

fn main() {
    let secret_number = rand::thread_rng().gen_range(1..=100);
    println!("Your onboarding ID is: {}", secret_number);
}
```

Do not worry if the syntax on the first few lines looks unfamiliar—we will cover variables and importing libraries in later chapters. The key takeaway is the workflow. 

Run the program with `cargo run`. You will see output similar to this:

```text
    Updating crates.io index
  Downloaded rand v0.8.5
  Downloaded 1 crate (1.2 MB) in 0.5s
   Compiling libc v0.2.153
   Compiling cfg-if v1.0.0
   Compiling ppv-lite86 v0.2.17
   Compiling getrandom v0.2.12
   Compiling rand_core v0.6.4
   Compiling rand_chacha v0.2.2
   Compiling rand v0.8.5
   Compiling hello_rust v0.1.0 (/path/to/your/hello_rust)
    Finished dev [unoptimized + debuginfo] target(s) in 1.8s
     Running `target/debug/hello_rust`
Your onboarding ID is: 42
```

Notice what happened. When you typed `cargo run`, Cargo saw that you added `rand` to your `Cargo.toml`. It automatically reached out to **crates.io** (the official community registry of Rust libraries), downloaded the `rand` library, downloaded any libraries that `rand` itself relies on, compiled all of them, and finally compiled and ran your program. 

This is the magic of Cargo. You never have to manually download zip files, configure include paths, or struggle with linker errors. You simply add a line to `Cargo.toml`, and Cargo handles the rest.

## Key Takeaways

*   **Rustup** is the official installer and version manager for Rust. You verify it is installed by checking `rustc --version`.
*   **Cargo** is Rust’s built-in build tool and package manager. It is the standard way to create, build, and manage Rust projects.
*   Running `cargo new <project_name>` generates a standardized project structure, including a `Cargo.toml` configuration file and a `src/main.rs` file for your code.
*   The `main` function inside `main.rs` is the entry point for every Rust program. The `println!` macro is used to print text to the screen.
*   `cargo run` compiles and executes your code in one step. `cargo build` compiles without running, and `cargo check` rapidly checks for errors without generating an executable.
*   `cargo fmt` automatically formats your code to standard Rust style guidelines.
*   External libraries (called crates) are added to your project by listing them in the `[dependencies]` section of your `Cargo.toml` file. Cargo automatically downloads and compiles them for you.

---

# Chapter 2: Variables, Data Types & Operators

## Storing and Changing Data

Every program you write will manipulate data. Whether you are calculating the total price of a shopping cart, checking if a user is logged in, or processing text from a file, you need ways to store and manipulate information. In Rust, we store data in **variables**.

In the previous chapter, we used Cargo to build and run a program that printed `"Hello, world!"`. We did this using the `println!` macro. But what if we want to reuse a piece of data multiple times, or change it before printing? 

Let's look at a simple scenario. Imagine you are building a program to track the lives of a character in a game. You start by creating a variable named `health` and assigning it a value of `100`.

```rust
fn main() {
    let health = 100;
    println!("Player health: {}", health);
}
```

Here, we use the `let` keyword to declare (create) a variable. We name it `health`, use the equals sign `=` to assign it the value `100`, and end the statement with a semicolon `;`. Finally, we use the `{}` syntax inside the `println!` macro to insert the value of our variable into the text.

If you run this code using `cargo run`, it will output `Player health: 100`.

## The Power of Immutability

If you write a lot of Python, JavaScript, or C++, you might expect to be able to change the value of `health` simply by assigning a new value to it. Let's try subtracting 20 points of damage when the player gets hit:

```rust
fn main() {
    let health = 100;
    health = health - 20; // The player takes 20 damage

    println!("Player health: {}", health);
}
```

If you try to compile this, the Rust compiler will stop you in your tracks with an error:

```text
error[E0384]: cannot assign twice to immutable variable `health`
 --> src/main.rs:3:5
  |
2 |     let health = 100;
  |         ------ first assignment to `health`
3 |     health = health - 20;
  |     ^^^^^ cannot assign twice to immutable variable
```

This is one of the most defining features of Rust: **variables are immutable by default**. 

When a variable is **immutable**, once a value is bound to a name, you cannot change that value. Rust enforces this rule at compile time. Why? Because human error is the root of countless bugs. If a variable can be changed anywhere in a large program, it becomes incredibly difficult to track how and when its value changes. By forcing variables to be immutable by default, Rust allows the compiler to guarantee that a specific piece of data will not change unexpectedly, making your code inherently safer and easier to reason about.

### Making Variables Mutable

Of course, sometimes you *do* need to change a value. In a game, a player's health fluctuates constantly. To tell the Rust compiler that a variable is allowed to change, you use the `mut` keyword.

```rust
fn main() {
    let mut health = 100; // We add 'mut' here
    health = health - 20; // This is now perfectly legal

    println!("Player health: {}", health);
}
```

Now the program compiles successfully and outputs `Player health: 80`. 

By requiring you to explicitly write `mut`, Rust forces you to declare your intent. If you read through a block of Rust code and see a variable declared without `mut`, you can immediately breathe a sigh of relief—you know for a fact that its value will never change while the program runs.

## Primitive Data Types

Every value in Rust is of a certain **data type**, which tells the compiler what kind of data is being stored and what operations can be performed on it. Rust is a **statically typed** language, meaning it must know the types of all variables at compile time. 

Rust has several built-in primitive types, which are the most basic building blocks of the language. Let's look at the four most common ones.

### 1. Integers

An **integer** is a number without a fractional component. In our `health` example, we used an integer. Rust provides several different integer types based on two characteristics: whether they can be negative, and their size in memory.

- **Signed integers** can store both positive and negative numbers. They start with `i` (for integer). Examples: `i8`, `i16`, `i32`, `i64`, `i128`.
- **Unsigned integers** can only store positive numbers (and zero). They start with `u` (for unsigned). Examples: `u8`, `u16`, `u32`, `u64`, `u128`.

The number after the `i` or `u` represents how many bits of memory the number takes up. An `i8` can store numbers from -128 to 127, while a `u8` can store numbers from 0 to 255. 

If you aren't sure which one to use, Rust defaults to `i32`, which is generally the fastest on modern CPUs, even on 64-bit systems.

### 2. Floating-Point Numbers

**Floating-point numbers** are numbers with decimal points. Rust has two floating-point types: `f32` and `f64`. The default is `f64` because on modern CPUs it is roughly the same speed as `f32` but offers more precision.

```rust
let price = 19.99; // Defaults to f64
let tax_rate: f32 = 0.08; // Explicitly a 32-bit float
```

### 3. Booleans

A **boolean** type in Rust can be one of two values: `true` or `false`. Booleans are exactly one byte in size and are declared using the `bool` keyword. They are incredibly useful for keeping track of state, such as whether a user is logged in or a game is paused.

```rust
let is_logged_in = true;
let game_over = false;
```

### 4. Characters

Rust supports a **character** type called `char`. It represents a single Unicode Scalar Value, meaning it can represent far more than just ASCII letters. You can store emojis, accented letters, and characters from non-Latin alphabets. `char` literals are specified with single quotes.

```rust
let first_initial = 'A';
let omega = 'Ω';
let sparkle = '✨';
```

*Note: Do not confuse the `char` type with a string literal like `"Hello, world!"`. A `char` is a single Unicode character wrapped in single quotes, while a string literal is text wrapped in double quotes. We will cover strings in depth in a later chapter.*

## Type Inference and Annotations

If Rust is statically typed and must know all types at compile time, how did our very first example (`let health = 100;`) compile without us telling Rust it was an integer?

This is thanks to **type inference**. The Rust compiler is very smart. When it sees `let health = 100;`, it looks at the value `100`, realizes it is a whole number, and automatically infers the type as `i32` (the default integer type). 

However, there are times when the compiler cannot infer the type, or when you want to force a specific type for performance or memory reasons. In these cases, you use an **explicit type annotation**. You do this by adding a colon `:` followed by the type name after the variable name.

```rust
fn main() {
    // Let the compiler infer the type (defaults to f64)
    let inferred_pi = 3.14159; 

    // Explicitly tell Rust this is a 32-bit float
    let explicit_pi: f32 = 3.14159; 

    // Explicitly declare an unsigned 8-bit integer
    let player_level: u8 = 50;
}
```

## Variable Shadowing

Rust has a unique feature called **shadowing**. When you declare a new variable with the same name as an existing variable using the `let` keyword, the new variable *shadows* the old one. The old variable is no longer accessible by that name.

You might wonder: *Isn't this just the same as making a variable `mut`?* Not quite. When you use `mut`, you are allowed to change the value of the variable, but you cannot change its type. Shadowing, on the other hand, creates an entirely new variable, which means you can change the type while reusing the name.

A common real-world use case for shadowing is converting data from one type to another. Imagine you are building a user interface and you ask the user for their age. User input is always read as text (a string), but you want to perform math on it to see if they are over 18. 

```rust
fn main() {
    // Pretend we received this from user input. 
    // We use a string literal here for simplicity.
    let user_input = "25"; 

    // We want to convert this text into an actual number.
    // We shadow the `user_input` variable to reuse the name.
    let user_input: i32 = user_input.trim().parse().unwrap();

    // Now `user_input` is an integer, and we can do math with it
    let next_year_age = user_input + 1;
    
    println!("Next year you will be {}", next_year_age);
}
```

*(Don't worry about the `.trim().parse().unwrap()` syntax for now; we will cover how to handle user input and potential errors in later chapters. The key takeaway is that `user_input` started as text and was shadowed into an integer using `let`.)*

## Operators

Now that we know how to store data, we need to manipulate it. **Operators** are special symbols that perform specific operations on one or more values (called operands). Rust supports several categories of operators.

### Arithmetic Operators

These are used for basic mathematical calculations. 

- `+` : Addition
- `-` : Subtraction
- `*` : Multiplication
- `/` : Division
- `%` : Modulo (returns the remainder of a division)

```rust
fn main() {
    let sum = 5 + 10;
    let difference = 95.5 - 5.0; // Floating point math
    let product = 4 * 30;
    let quotient = 56 / 32;     // Integer division results in 1
    let remainder = 43 % 5;     // 43 divided by 5 is 8, remainder 3

    println!("Sum: {}", sum);
    println!("Remainder: {}", remainder);
}
```

**A critical rule for beginners:** Rust does not automatically convert between different numeric types. You cannot add an `i32` and an `f64` together directly. If you try `let broken_math = 5 + 2.5;`, the compiler will throw a type mismatch error. Both operands must be the same type.

### Comparison Operators

Comparison operators evaluate two values and return a **boolean** (`true` or `false`). These are essential for making decisions in your code (which we will cover in the next chapter on Control Flow).

- `==` : Equal to
- `!=` : Not equal to
- `>` : Greater than
- `<` : Less than
- `>=` : Greater than or equal to
- `<=` : Less than or equal to

```rust
fn main() {
    let player_score = 1500;
    let high_score = 2000;

    let is_new_high_score = player_score > high_score; // false
    let is_tied = player_score == high_score;          // false

    println!("New high score? {}", is_new_high_score);
}
```

### Logical Operators

Logical operators allow you to combine multiple boolean values or invert them. 

- `&&` : Logical AND (returns `true` only if *both* sides are true)
- `||` : Logical OR (returns `true` if *either* side is true)
- `!` : Logical NOT (inverts a boolean value)

Imagine a banking app that checks if a user can withdraw money. They need to have enough funds *and* their account must not be frozen.

```rust
fn main() {
    let account_balance = 500;
    let withdrawal_amount = 100;
    let is_account_frozen = false;

    // The user can withdraw if they have enough money AND the account is not frozen
    let can_withdraw = (account_balance >= withdrawal_amount) && !is_account_frozen;

    println!("Can withdraw funds? {}", can_withdraw); // Prints true
}
```

## Putting It Together: A Real-World Calculation

Let's combine variables, mutability, types, and operators into a practical example. Imagine you are calculating the total cost of an online shopping cart. The cart holds items, the user has a discount code, and we need to calculate the final price including tax.

```rust
fn main() {
    // Explicitly define the item price and quantity
    let item_price: f64 = 29.99;
    let quantity: u32 = 3;

    // Calculate the subtotal
    let mut subtotal = item_price * (quantity as f64);
    println!("Subtotal: ${}", subtotal);

    // Apply a 10% discount
    let discount_rate = 0.10;
    let discount_amount = subtotal * discount_rate;
    
    // Subtract the discount from the subtotal (requires mut)
    subtotal = subtotal - discount_amount;
    println!("After discount: ${}", subtotal);

    // Calculate tax (8%)
    let tax_rate: f64 = 0.08;
    let tax_amount = subtotal * tax_rate;

    // Calculate final total using addition
    let final_total = subtotal + tax_amount;
    
    println!("Tax: ${}", tax_amount);
    println!("Final Total: ${}", final_total);
}
```

*Note: In the code above, we used `(quantity as f64)`. This is called **casting**. Because `quantity` is a `u32` integer and `item_price` is an `f64` float, Rust will not let us multiply them directly. We temporarily cast the integer into a float just for this math operation so the types match.*

## Key Takeaways

- Use the `let` keyword to declare variables. Variables in Rust are **immutable by default**, meaning their values cannot be changed once assigned.
- Use the `mut` keyword (e.g., `let mut x = 5;`) to create a mutable variable. Rust requires you to be explicit about your intent to change data.
- Rust has four core primitive types: **integers** (`i32`, `u8`), **floating-point numbers** (`f64`, `f32`), **booleans** (`bool`), and **characters** (`char`).
- The compiler can usually figure out a variable's type automatically via **type inference**, but you can provide an **explicit type annotation** (e.g., `let x: i32 = 5;`) when necessary.
- **Shadowing** allows you to declare a new variable with the same name as an existing one, effectively overwriting it. This is useful for transforming a variable's value or changing its type without coming up with a new name.
- **Arithmetic** (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`), **comparison** (`==`, `!=`, `>`, `<`), and **logical** (`&&`, `||`, `!`) operators allow you to perform calculations and evaluate conditions.
- Rust strictly enforces types. You cannot perform math operations (like adding an integer to a float) on mismatched types without explicitly converting one of them.
